headline: CORN HOLDS GAINS AS BEANS EDGE HIGHER
subheadline: Markets consolidate after Monday's planting scare as weather watch intensifies.
lead: Corn held steady at $4.59 after Monday's weather-driven rally, while soybeans added 2.75 cents to $11.87 on continued South American tightness. Livestock struggled with feeder cattle dropping 1.1% as producers face margin pressure.
teaser: Grain markets consolidate gains while cattle producers feel the squeeze.

one_number.context: Feeder cattle dropped to $361.35, down 1.1% as the gap between corn input costs and cattle prices continues to widen. With corn holding near $4.60 and feeders struggling to hold $360, the math for cattle feeding operations is getting tougher by the day.

sections.title: Grains Hold Steady
sections.body: Corn futures stayed flat at $4.59 as traders digested Monday's weather premium. December corn at $4.79 shows the market still expects seasonal pressure once planting wraps up. Soybeans gained modestly with nearby futures at $11.87 and November at $11.62, supported by tight South American supplies. Chicago wheat dipped to $6.05 as spring planting conditions look favorable across the Northern Plains.
sections.bottom_line: Grain markets pause after Monday's weather scare, holding recent gains.

sections.title: Livestock Divergence
sections.body: Feeder cattle fell hardest, dropping 1.1% to $361.35 as the spread between input costs and cattle prices widens. Live cattle at $246.07 held better but still closed lower on margin concerns. Lean hogs bucked the trend, gaining 0.7% to $101.72 on stronger cash markets and tight supplies. Class III milk dropped 1.5% to $16.86 as spring flush season approaches.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle struggle with feed costs while hogs find support from tight supplies.

sections.title: Energy Steady
sections.body: WTI crude oil added 0.2% to $86.88, holding near recent highs but off Monday's surge. Natural gas gained 0.5% to $2.67 as spring demand patterns emerge. Diesel margins remain elevated with refinery capacity still constrained heading into planting season when fuel demand typically peaks.
sections.bottom_line: Energy markets calm after Monday's volatility, diesel stays expensive.

the_more_you_know.title: Understanding Feeder Cattle Margins
the_more_you_know.body: When feeder cattle futures trade at $361 and corn sits near $4.60, the math gets ugly fast. A 750-pound feeder costs roughly $2,700 today. Add 2,800 pounds of corn at current prices (about $320) plus other costs, and you need live cattle above $250 to break even. That's a thin margin with live cattle at $246. This spread compression is why feedlots slow purchases and why feeder prices drop hardest when corn stays elevated.

daily_quote.text: In God we trust. All others must bring data.

source_summary: CME Group, USDA, EIA, Federal Reserve
